1: Psalm 70 is a doublet of Ps 40:13-17 — the final five verses of Ps 40 appear here as a standalone emergency prayer. The main differences: Ps 70 uses Elohim where Ps 40 uses YHWH, and Ps 70:5 reads slightly differently from Ps 40:17. The superscription "to bring to remembrance (hazkîr)" — also in Ps 38 — probably refers to the azkārāh, a memorial portion of grain offering (Lev 2:2, 9, 16) associated with prayer and remembrance before God. Liturgically, Ps 70 was likely used as a standalone petition of urgency — an abbreviated form of Ps 40 stripped to its essential plea, extracted for use in acute crisis. The echo data for this psalm notes connections to Heb 5:7 (Jesus's urgent prayer) and Matt 26:39 (Gethsemane).
2: "Let those who seek my life be put to shame and confusion; let those who delight in my harm be turned back and disgraced" — the defensive petition: the reversal of the attackers' plans. "Shame and confusion (yēbōšû wĕyaḥpĕrû)" — both terms are honor-shame vocabulary: bōš (be ashamed, suffer disgrace) and ḥāpar (be ashamed, be in confusion). "Those who delight in my harm (ḥāpēṣ rāʿātî)" — enemies whose active pleasure is the psalmist's destruction, not merely incidental opposition. The prayer asks that their delight be converted into disgrace — the precise inversion of their intentions.
3: "Let them be turned back in their shame — those who say, 'Aha, aha! (heʾāḥ heʾāḥ)!'" — the mocking exclamation heʾāḥ (aha! / ha ha!) is the verbal expression of schadenfreude — gloating at another's suffering. It appears in Pss 35:21, 25; 40:15; Ezek 25:3; 26:2 — always in the mouth of enemies who take pleasure in the fall of Israel or her king. The prayer is not merely that enemies fail but that those who gloated be specifically humiliated — turned back (yāšûbû) in the shame they wanted to impose on the psalmist. The mirror of justice: the emotion they expressed becomes their experience.
4: "But let all who seek you rejoice and be glad in you; let those who love your salvation say always, 'God is great (yigdal ʾĕlōhîm)!'" — the communal positive petition contrasts sharply with vv. 2-3. Those who seek YHWH are invited into joy; those who love his salvation (yĕšûʿāh) are to perpetually proclaim his greatness. "God is great" (yigdal ʾĕlōhîm) — the continuous, perpetual (tāmîd) acclamation: an ongoing liturgical posture of acknowledging divine majesty. Paul echoes the spirit of this verse in Phil 4:4 ("Rejoice in the Lord always; I will say it again, rejoice!"), applying the same perpetual joy to the community of those who love God's salvation.
5: "But I am poor and needy (ʿānî wĕʾebyôn); hurry to me, O God! You are my help and my deliverer; O LORD, do not tarry (ʾal-tĕʾaḥar)" — the compressed closing verse brings together the standard self-designation of the Psalter's petitioner (ʿānî wĕʾebyôn — poor and needy; cf. Ps 40:17; 69:29; 86:1) with the most urgent petition of the psalm: "do not tarry." The urgency throughout Ps 70 is exceptional even for the lament genre — "hurry to deliver" (v. 1), "make haste to help" (v. 1), "hurry to me" (v. 5), "do not tarry" (v. 5). Time itself is the critical variable; divine delay is the specific fear. The psalm is the concentrated essence of crisis prayer.
